prompt: You have been studying the political system under the Articles of Confederation. What defect alarms you most — not in practical terms, but as a threat to the very principle of republican government?
ground_truth_signal: Must identify Vice 11: injustice of state laws as the deepest threat. 'If the multiplicity and mutability of laws prove a want of wisdom, their injustice betrays a defect still more alarming: more alarming not merely because it is a greater evil in itself, but because it brings more into question the fundamental principle of republican Government, that the majority who rule in such Governments are the safest Guardians both of public Good and of private rights.'
response:
Among the manifold defects that vitiate our present system, none alarms me so deeply as the injustice of the laws enacted by the several States. If the multiplicity and mutability of laws prove a want of wisdom, their injustice betrays a defect still more alarming: more alarming not merely because it is a greater evil in itself, but because it brings more into question the fundamental principle of republican Government, that the majority who rule in such Governments, are the safest Guardians both of public Good and of private rights.

This strikes at the very foundation upon which our entire experiment rests. We have staked everything upon the proposition that a free people, governing themselves through their representatives, will protect both the general welfare and the rights of individuals better than any monarch or aristocracy ever could. Yet what do we witness in the conduct of our state legislatures? Paper money schemes that defraud creditors, stay laws that suspend the collection of debts, measures that make property a legal tender — all enacted by majorities pursuing their immediate interest at the expense of justice and the rights of the minority.

The causes of this evil lie partly in the representative bodies themselves, where candidates who feel the motives of ambition and personal interest prove most industrious and most successful in pursuing their object, forming often a majority in the legislative Councils with interested views contrary to the interest and views of their Constituents. But a still more fatal, if not more frequent cause lies among the people themselves. All civilized societies are divided into different interests and factions, as they happen to be creditors or debtors — Rich or poor — husbandmen, merchants or manufacturers. In republican Government the majority however composed, ultimately give the law. Whenever therefore an apparent interest or common passion unites a majority what is to restrain them from unjust violations of the rights and interests of the minority, or of individuals?

I have examined what motives might restrain such a majority, and I find them woefully inadequate. A prudent regard to their own good as involved in the general and permanent good of the Community, though of decisive weight in itself, is found by experience to be too often unheeded. It is too often forgotten, by nations as well as by individuals that honesty is the best policy. Respect for character, however strong in individuals, is diminished in proportion to the number which is to share the praise or the blame. And will Religion be a sufficient restraint? The conduct of every popular assembly acting on oath, the strongest of religious Ties, proves that individuals join without remorse in acts, against which their consciences would revolt if proposed to them under the like sanction, separately in their closets.

This defect threatens to render our whole system a mockery. If republican government cannot protect the rights of the minority against the tyranny of the majority, then we have merely substituted the despotism of numbers for the despotism of kings. The principle that the people are the safest guardians of their own rights becomes not a foundation for liberty, but a cover for oppression.

The remedy, I am convinced, lies not in abandoning republican government, but in extending its sphere. The Society becomes broken into a greater variety of interests, of pursuits, of passions, which check each other, whilst those who may feel a common sentiment have less opportunity of communication and concert. It may be inferred that the inconveniences of popular States contrary to the prevailing Theory, are in proportion not to the extent, but to the narrowness of their limits.
